I.title: The Spring Retreat
I.subtitle: Contemplation · March 27–29, 2026 · CT1–CT6
I.bio: Pir Zia Inayat-Khan is the spiritual leader of the Inayatiyya and grandson of Hazrat Inayat Khan. He taught Section I of the Contemplation program (CT1–CT10) across two retreat weekends: the Spring Retreat of March 27–29, 2026 (CT1–CT6) and the Easter Retreat of April 18–19, 2026 (CT7–CT10).

1.title: Prayers & Invocations
1.subtitle: The Sacred Words That Open the Way
1.intro: Contemplation opens, as the Suluk opens, with prayer. Pir Zia taught the foundational invocations of the Inayatiyya — the Opening Invocation, Saum, Salat, Salawat, and the prayers of the lineage — as the living liturgy within which every contemplation is framed.

snippets.title: The Invocation — Opening of Contemplation
snippets.body:
# The Invocation — Opening of Contemplation

"Toward the One, the perfection of love, harmony and beauty, the only being, united with all of the illuminated souls who form the embodiment of the messenger, the spirit of guidance."

The Invocation opens the first session of the Contemplation series, marking the formal transition from the completed Book of Concentration into the new stage of the Suluk journey. It is the first act of contemplative practice — gathering scattered attention into unified intention.

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*
snippets.session: Suluk Academy 2026 Contemplation P1 Class 1 / March 27, 2026 / Pir Zia Inayat-Khan

snippets.title: Creating the Realm of Sanctuary (Dar al-Aman)
snippets.body: In a world marked by polarization, militarism, and suffering, the spiritual path calls for a conscious response. We must acknowledge that humanity is one family, and what affects the most vulnerable ultimately affects us all. While we cannot control global events, we can actively cultivate a space of refuge and peace in our immediate environment. This practice begins not externally, but in the interior life. The teaching of Aman, or sanctuary, is a powerful tool for this work. "Our intention is to create the Dar al-Aman, the realm of sanctuary, beginning in our own hearts." This inner sanctuary is built through the consideration we extend to all of God's creatures, the kindness we show one another, and the intention to make every thought, word, and action a building block in a temple of peace. It is a commitment to embody kindredness and demonstrate a different way of being in the world.

snippets.title: The Inayati Opening Prayer
snippets.body: In the Inayatiyya, we initiate our sacred gatherings with a profound invocation, a calling forth of the divine presence and the stream of spiritual wisdom. This prayer, steeped in tradition, sets the essential tone for our shared contemplation and practice. It reminds us of our ultimate destination and our connection to a lineage of enlightened understanding. We begin by reciting: "Toward the one, the perfection of love, harmony, and beauty. The only being, united with all of the illuminated souls who form the embodiment of the messenger, the spirit of guidance." This declaration is a compass for our hearts, orienting us towards the singular source of all existence—the perfection of love, harmony, and beauty—and uniting us with the continuous spiritual guidance embodied by the messengers of truth.

snippets.title: The Practice of Ittisal: Conjunction with Universal Being
snippets.body: The practice of Ittisal, which means conjunction, aims for a profound connection with the universal being—the total being of the universe, also known as the spirit of guidance. The physical cosmos is understood as the body of this universal being, and our individual bodies are merely cells within this vaster organism. Similarly, our minds are a portion of the universal mind, and beyond that lies a universal soul. By consciously tapping into this larger body, mind, and soul, we can be nourished, guided, and inspired, gaining access to knowledge and perception beyond our personal senses. "The more that we breathe with the universe as a cell within that larger body, the more that we become connected with life itself, the more we are guided and and the more that we're able to contribute to the the coherence of the anatomy that is unfolding itself around us." This practice is an awakening to our inherent rapport with this vaster reality, fostering a sense of belonging and coherence with all of life.

snippets.title: Energizing Ittisal with the Intonation of 'Ha'
snippets.body: The practice of Ittisal is energized through a series of intonations, the first of which is the syllable "Ha." This sound is felt rising from the coccyx, or tailbone, and moving up into the chest, where its vibration culminates. This practice helps one feel how the physical and subtle bodies are embedded within the universal dimensions, like a vortex or whirlpool of cosmic substance. It cultivates a profound shift in perspective, moving from the idea of a separate self living *in* the universe to the realization that the universe is an extension of our own being. "Normally we think, well, this body is mine and I live in the universe. But you could equally say, this is my little body and the universe is my great body." The intonation of "Ha" is a method to physically and energetically experience this deep connection, realizing the universe as our larger, great body and ourselves as a dynamic part of its continuous flow.

snippets.title: Al-Abd al-Kulli: The Universal Servant
snippets.body: The universal being, the total being of the universe, has been given many names throughout history. One particularly evocative name is Al-Abd al-Kulli, which translates to 'the universal servant' or 'worshiper of God.' This offers a profound way of conceiving the cosmos, suggesting that the entirety of creation, in all its diversity, collectively forms a single, primal devotee of the infinite. "This whole world, all the way out to its farthest shores, constitutes an entity who exists for the worship and witnessing of the divine being." In this view, the sum total of all creatures constitutes this universal servant. Each individual being, including ourselves, is therefore a holographic fraction of this grand, universal totality, participating in its continuous act of devotion and witness.

snippets.title: The 'Ha' Intonation as a Bija Mantra
snippets.body: The intonation of 'Ha' is not merely a sound but a practice rooted in ancient traditions of sacred sound. This can be understood by comparing it to similar concepts in other spiritual languages. For instance, in Arabic, there are three primary vowelizations—Fathah, Dammah, and Kasrah—that modulate consonants. In a similar vein, the 'Ha' sound functions like what is known in the Indic tradition as a 'bija mantra' or seed sound. "in, in the Indic tradition, you would speak of bija mantras. They aren't even words, they're just essential sounds, and they resonate in particular places in the body." The power of 'Ha' lies in this resonance; it is an essential sound that rises from the coccyx, or tailbone, and culminates with a powerful vibration in the chest, physically connecting the practitioner to a deeper energetic reality.

snippets.title: The Vigilant Practice of Muraqaba
snippets.body: The practice of contemplation, or Muraqaba, is deeply connected to the divine name Raqib, which means the Vigilant or the Watchful. When we engage in Muraqaba, we are not inventing a new state but rather participating in a divine quality that already permeates all of existence with its attentive, watchful care. It is an invitation to shift from a state of sleepwalking through our days to one of profound wakefulness. This practice asks, "What would it be like not merely to stumble through life, not merely to sleepwalk, but to take every step wakefully, vigilantly?" It involves taking every encounter, every object, and every moment seriously, recognizing the divine signs and presence within them. Instead of life being a series of accidental events, it becomes a field of meaning where we are called to be present, attentive, and deeply engaged with the reality unfolding before us and within us.

snippets.title: I'tibar: Contemplation as an Act of Respect
snippets.body: Another crucial term for contemplation is I'tibar, a word that also means 'respect.' This connection implies that true contemplation is an act of paying deep respect to everyone and everything, taking nothing for granted. We are invited to see deeply into the source of all things, recognizing that nothing is merely a reflective surface; everything possesses infinite depth. The root of I'tibar connects to 'abara, meaning 'to cross over.' In contemplation, we cross from the surface of an experience into its profound depth, finding a porousness in all things. Through this respectful crossing-over, "Objects become subjects, subjects not only meet but pass through each other, wade into each other. And in this way we derive 'ibara, which means meaning from the same root." Meaning arises when we move with respect from the outer evidence to the inner significance, and ultimately to the divine name that is its origin.

snippets.title: Ta'bir: The Interpretation of Dreams
snippets.body: A concept related to I'tibar (contemplation) is Ta'bir, which shares the same root and signifies interpretation, particularly the interpretation of dreams. While one might wake from a dream and immediately forget it, a vigilant and watchful person will linger, recollecting the experience. This is where I'tibar comes in; it is the act of sensing the deeper meaning behind the dream's narrative. It is not just about remembering events or figures but perceiving the signification and hints embedded within them. As Pir Zia explains, "if you have I'tibar, then you not only remember there was this incident and this person appeared, but you sense behind the narrative a meaning, a signification, things are hinted at that you are meant to know, but you have to linger over it." This practice of Ta'bir, therefore, is an extension of contemplation, applying respectful, deep attention to the symbolic language of the inner world to uncover knowledge meant for the soul.

snippets.title: The Personal Nature of Dream Interpretation
snippets.body: While the interpretation of dreams (Ta'bir) seeks deeper meaning, it is not a mechanical process of looking up symbols. There are historical resources, like the famous dream dictionary written by Ibn Sirin, but these should be used with caution. The meaning of a symbol is deeply personal and contextual. As the teaching clarifies, "Ibn Sirin wrote a dream dictionary, but one symbol doesn't mean the same thing for every dreamer." While shared culture provides common resonances for symbols, the ultimate interpretation is a unique and personal experience. An interpreter can act as a guide, but their role is simply to help the dreamer access the knowledge that is already present within their own being. True understanding is not found in a book but is obtained through the contemplative act of I'tibar, by going deeper than the surface of the dream's imagery to find its personal significance.

snippets.title: The Opening Invocation
snippets.body: We begin our practice of contemplation by turning our hearts and minds to the Divine Source. In this sacred endeavor, we offer ourselves to the boundless reality that encompasses all existence, a reality described by the invocation: "Toward the One, the perfection of love, harmony, and beauty, the only being, united with all the illuminated souls, who form the embodiment of the messenger, the spirit of guidance." This invocation guides us to remember that contemplation is an active participation in the divine quality of attentiveness and watchful care. It is a journey of seeing deeply into everyone and everything, recognizing the divine presence within, and ultimately, understanding that our purpose is to witness and embody these very qualities, bringing meaning and light into our lives.

snippets.title: The Spiritual Rhythm of Opening, Bestowal, and Patience
snippets.body: The Wazifa of Ya Fattah, Ya Wahhab, and Ya Sabur outlines a fundamental rhythm of the spiritual life. These three divine names are not isolated qualities but form a dynamic and profound sequence. The process begins with Ya Sabur, the Patient, which is not a passive waiting but an active, vibrant steadfastness in our intention and practice. This endurance prepares the ground for a divine intervention. We then turn to Ya Fattah, the Opener, with a prayer to remove the obstacles and clear the constrictions in our hearts and lives. Following this opening, the grace of Ya Wahhab, the Bestower, can flow freely. "These three divine names form a sequence that describes a profound spiritual process. Ya Fattah is the Opener, the one who removes obstacles and opens the way. Ya Wahhab is the Bestower, the Giver of gifts, the one who bestows grace without measure. And Ya Sabur is the Patient, the one who is steadfast and endures." This cycle of patient waiting, a sudden opening, and a generous outpouring of grace unfolds according to a divine wisdom that is beyond our limited comprehension, guiding the soul's journey.

snippets.title: Gratitude for Constriction: Deepening Spiritual Roots
snippets.body: A mature spiritual perspective involves cultivating gratitude not just for moments of blissful opening and generous bestowal, but also for the inevitable periods of waiting, difficulty, and constriction. These challenging times are not a sign that we have been abandoned or that we are failing in our practice. On the contrary, they are an integral and necessary part of the soul's growth. It is during these phases of patient endurance that our spiritual roots are forced to grow deeper, strengthening our foundation and expanding our capacity to receive grace when it comes. "Let us cultivate gratitude not only for the moments of opening and bestowal but also for the periods of waiting and patient endurance. The times of constriction and difficulty are not a sign of divine displeasure; they are an essential part of the process." By embracing both expansion and contraction as expressions of the one Beloved's wisdom, we develop a resilient and unwavering trust in the path.

snippets.title: A Closing Prayer for Opening, Grace, and Patience
snippets.body: We practice with the Wazifa, Ya Fattah, Ya Wahhab, Ya Sabur, which describes a profound spiritual process—a rhythm of patient waiting, sudden opening, and generous outpouring of grace. In concluding our practice, we offer a prayer, giving thanks for the opportunity to sit in remembrance and for whatever has been received, seen or unseen, as we ask: "May the Opener open our hearts. May the Bestower fill them with His love. And may the Patient One grant us the steadfastness to remain on this path until we reach our true home in Him." This prayer encapsulates our profound trust and surrender, aligning our will with the Divine as we patiently await the unveiling of truth and the ultimate journey home in Him.

snippets.title: The Swing of Breath: Rising Above Birth and Death
snippets.body: A foundational practice in contemplation involves attuning to the rhythm of the breath, transforming it into a vehicle for spiritual ascent. The process begins by simply watching the breath as it moves, a natural, effortless cycle. "As the breath goes out far and comes in deep, the effect is that of a swing, going out, falling back, going out, falling back." Upon this rhythmic swing, a Fikr, or remembrance, is placed. The phrase for this practice is, on the inhale, "I rise above birth and death," and on the exhale, "through the waves of the air." This is not merely a mental recitation but an experiential immersion. The goal is to live the words, to feel the sensation of floating, gliding, and soaring beyond the ordinary confines of existence. The practice culminates in a state of dual awareness: maintaining the experience of being beyond birth and death while simultaneously remaining grounded and present in the physical body, in the here and now.

snippets.title: Meditation: Entering Dreamless Sleep Consciously
snippets.body: The study of the seven leading divine names is a journey into the very structure of the universe and the self. It is an exploration of our most ancient memory, a recollection of a time before time. This process is akin to the experience of waking from a dream and sensing the state that preceded it. As Pir Zia explains, "Behind one's conscious memories, one intuits a time before time, just as in the morning waking from a dream, one intuits a dreamless sleep preceding the dream." Meditation, in this context, is the art of consciously entering this state of formlessness, this dreamless sleep, while remaining fully wakeful. It is not about the dualistic consciousness of our everyday life but a deeper, more unified awareness. This profound exploration is what allows for the realization of the most primordial divine names, bringing us closer to the source of our own being and the cosmos itself.

snippets.title: The Essence and Practice of Wazifa: Chanting Ya Hayyu
snippets.body: The practice of Wazifa, the repetition of a divine name, is a core Sufi method for spiritual realization, moving beyond mere mechanical recitation. The true purpose is to immerse oneself completely in the quality that the name represents. When practicing a name like 'Ya Hayyu' (O Living One), the goal is to become one with the very essence of life itself. The instruction is to "dissolve into livingness. Let the divine name speak for itself and announce a quality of being that is so clear and unmistakable that in that moment, that is all there is." Following this explanation, the class engaged in a lengthy, chanted Zikr of 'Ya Hayyu,' putting the teaching into immediate practice. This deep absorption allows the practitioner to experience the divine attribute directly, not as an external concept but as an internal, all-encompassing reality, transforming consciousness through sound and devotion.

snippets.title: Opening Attunement
snippets.body: In the sacred space of contemplation, we begin by aligning ourselves with the divine source and the great stream of illumination. This opening invocation sets our intention and connects us to the universal spirit that underlies all existence. We say: "Toward the One, the perfection of love, harmony, and beauty, the only being, united with all the illuminated souls who form the embodiment of the messenger, the spirit of guidance." Having thus invoked the ultimate reality, we remain in a state of deep inwardness. We watch the breath as it goes out, spiraling far into the distance, and then as it changes direction and draws toward the interior. This practice helps us consciously connect with the continuous outpouring of the divine into all of creation and the unfolding of our own being from that very source, leading us to dissolve into its inherent livingness and wisdom.

snippets.title: The Spiritual Marriage of Opposites
snippets.body: We must be careful not to fall into a false dichotomy where light is good and darkness is bad. In creation, opposite poles contain something of each other and meet in a spiritual marriage, the coincidentia oppositorum. As the great Sufi al-Kharraz said, we should "look for God precisely where the opposites meet." Darkness is not merely the absence of light but a condition that reveals other modalities and finer transmissions of light, like the stars or the "midnight sun." The veiling of one light, the sun, allows other forms of light to be revealed. By filtering out the most obvious and glaring frequencies, we discover subtler luminosities. This is why Sufis speak of the midnight sun, a black sun, and the luminous manifestations that emerge in the shade. Just as closing our physical eyes is often necessary to attain inner vision, embracing darkness can lead to a more profound perception of light in its many gradations.

snippets.title: The Practice of Shughli Mahtabi and Light in Darkness
snippets.body: The session revisits the shughl, or practice, of sunlight and moonlight gazing. This involves looking at moonlight as it descends or sunlight shimmering on water, while taking care to protect the eyes. This exploration of light leads to a deeper principle found in the lunar practice, known as 'shughli mahtabi.' This principle demonstrates that what we perceive as opposites are deeply connected. For instance, the moon and especially the stars are most visible not in the bright light of day, but against the blackness of the night sky. "The veiling of the light of the sun enables other manifestations of light to be revealed." This shows that darkness is not an absence to be shunned, but a condition that allows for the perception of different, finer transmissions of light, revealing a more complex reality where opposites are not in conflict but in a complementary relationship.

snippets.title: The Prayer of All Holy Names and Forms
snippets.body: We understand that the Divine expresses itself in countless ways, appearing to us in varied forms and through many names. It is this universal presence we seek to recognize. In our prayer, we invoke this boundless recognition, saying: "Allow us to recognize thee in all thy holy names and forms. As in Sita and Rama, as in Radha and Krishna, as in Parvati and Shiva..." This aspiration extends to many other names, holy names and forms known and unknown to the world, including Tara and Buddha, Sarah, Hagar, and Abraham, Deborah and Solomon, Anahita and Zarathustra, Asiya, Aaron, Maryam and Moses, Mary and Jesus, Khadija and Muhammad. This practice reminds us that the singular divine essence animates all sacred relationships and archetypes, transcending cultural and religious boundaries. By embracing this truth, we foster a deep sense of unity in our hearts and perceive God's light shining in every aspect of creation.

2.title: Breath Practices
2.subtitle: The Rhythm That Carries the Contemplation
2.intro: The breath is the bridge between body and spirit, between the outer world and the inner chamber. These practices — purification breaths, elemental breaths, and the breath of Haq — prepare the ground on which every contemplation stands.

snippets.title: From Earth to Water: Concentration to Contemplation
snippets.body: The spiritual journey involves distinct stages of practice, which can be compared to the four elements. The initial stage, Concentration or Mujahada, is like the Earth element: heavy, dense, and slow. In a fast-moving world where attention is scarce, the first medicine is to learn stillness, to slow down and be fully present like a rock. This practice develops a rooted, stable consciousness. Having established this foundation, we now transition to the next stage, which corresponds to the Water element. This is the shift from concentration to contemplation, or Muraqaba. "Having been still, having been focused, focused on one object, now we're going to begin to move. The mind is going to begin to move." Unlike the random, reactive movement of an untrained mind, this is a graceful, artful, and purposeful unfolding, allowing us to explore the subtler dimensions of reality.

snippets.title: The Architecture of Meaning in Contemplation
snippets.body: Contemplation, or Muraqaba, moves beyond the stillness of concentration into a purposeful exploration of reality's deeper layers. After stabilizing the mind, we can allow it to move gracefully and artfully, not randomly reacting to stimuli but purposefully finding its way. This practice shifts our focus from the external forms of things to their inner essence. As Pir Zia explains, in contemplation, "we're not only going to be focusing on objects or even the forms of objects, but something subtler still, the quality of something, the character of something, the nature of it." This inquiry leads us into the very architecture of meaning that underlies the visible universe. We begin to perceive the fundamental qualities of being that all things manifest in various combinations. These qualities are, in their essence, the attributes of the Divine, and this perception opens a new way of seeing and understanding the world.

snippets.title: Contemplation: When the Object Holds the Mind
snippets.body: Hazrat Inayat Khan offers a crucial distinction between concentration and contemplation. In concentration, the mind actively holds an object; it is an outgoing act of grasping, like holding a frozen piece of ice. In contrast, contemplation, or Muraqaba, is a receptive state where one allows the object to hold the mind. This fundamental shift is summarized in the teaching: "In concentration, the mind holds an object. In contemplation, the object holds the mind." This moves the practitioner from an active, controlling state to one of allowing and receptivity. The object of attention is no longer a static 'thing' but becomes a subject, revealing itself and its deeper meanings. This process is like ice melting into flowing water that leads you somewhere new. Contemplation becomes a living encounter, a dialogue where we might be shown things we didn't expect, as the object unfolds its signs and speaks for itself.

snippets.title: The Shift from Objects to Living Ideas in Contemplation
snippets.body: A second principle of contemplation is that it focuses on an idea rather than a concrete object. This practice shifts our perception from seeing the world as a collection of fixed "things" to understanding it as a manifestation of living ideas and underlying patterns. Material forms are viewed as expressions of a consciousness that informs them. As Murshid says, "Contemplation is not on an object, it is on an idea." For example, when dealing with a difficult person, instead of focusing on the person as a fixed entity, one contemplates the mentality or qualities they express. This reveals that these patterns are not bounded by that individual and may even exist within oneself. This approach encourages us to see patterns, qualities, and currents of vibration rather than hermetically sealed monoliths, allowing us to work more consciously with the very constituents of consciousness itself.

snippets.title: Elevating Consciousness from the Dense World
snippets.body: The third indication on contemplation is its power to elevate consciousness. It is defined as the ability to retain an idea that lifts one's awareness from the dense, material world. Ideas act as "attractors of vibration," and our capacity to assimilate them depends on our preparedness, or *istidad*. This preparedness is like tuning a radio; the frequencies are always present, but we need the capacity to attune to and access them. The teaching states, "Contemplation is the ability to retain an idea which raises one's consciousness from the dense world." The process of spiritual unfoldment is about developing this capacity, reactivating finer modes of perception that have become dormant or vestigial due to our consciousness being so externalized. Through contemplation, we navigate and ascend through lattices of thought, moving from the bounded and concrete toward more fluid and vast modes of perception.

snippets.title: Navigating Malakut: The Lattices of Thought-Beings (Muwakkals)
snippets.body: In the practice of contemplation, one ascends through lattices of thought, moving toward vaster and finer capacities of perception. These lattices are formed by ideas, which are understood not as abstract concepts but as living formations of frequencies within the imaginal realm of Malakut. This realm is constituted by what are called 'muwakkals' or 'thought beings.' The teaching explains that an idea is "the formation of frequencies among the the muwakkals and among the the thought beings, the the particles of the world of Malakut." By engaging in contemplation, we navigate these intricate lattices, which allows us to reactivate finer modes of perception that have become dormant or vestigial. This process is an awakening of our consciousness from its externalized and determined state, allowing for a more profound engagement with the subtle realities of being.

snippets.title: Ya Fattah: The Divine Opener Beyond Our Will
snippets.body: The spiritual quality of Ya Fattah, the Opener, emphasizes the necessity of surrender on the path. Often, we may feel blocked, constricted, or shut down, and our own efforts to force an opening are futile. The practice of calling upon the Opener is an admission that true transformation comes from a source beyond our egoic will. It is an act of trust, a prayer for divine intervention to clear the way. The text uses the analogy of a flower bud that cannot force itself to bloom but must wait for the sun. "When we call upon Ya Fattah, we are acknowledging that the opening we seek is not something we can engineer through our own will. We may feel constricted, blocked, or shut down in our hearts and in our lives." This opening, whether in our external circumstances or our inner world, is the prerequisite for receiving the unearned, unconditional grace of Ya Wahhab, the Bestower. It is a shift from striving to receptivity, allowing a higher wisdom to guide the process of our unfolding.

snippets.title: A Breathing Practice for the Wazifa
snippets.body: As we engage in the silent Zikr of the Wazifa, the practice of Hosh dar dam, or awareness in the breath, serves as a vital anchor. By bringing our awareness to the breath, we ground ourselves in the living presence, allowing the divine names to permeate our being more deeply and safely. "The silent Zikr of the Wazifa can be synchronized with the rhythm of the breath. One might recite Ya Fattah on the in-breath, Ya Wahhab at the turning point, and Ya Sabur on the out-breath. Or one might find a different rhythm that feels natural." The key is to let the names and the breath merge into a single, continuous flow of remembrance, carrying the heart toward the ocean of unity. This integration deepens our attunement to these profound divine qualities, fostering openness, grace, and steadfastness in our lives.

snippets.title: The Complete Planes of Being: From Unmanifest to Human Form
snippets.body: Sufi cosmology describes creation as a continuous unfolding through several planes of being. It begins with Ahadiyat, or Essential Oneness, which is the Divine Essence (Zat-i-Ilahi), a reality 'beyond the beyond' (waral wara). The Divine Essence (Zat) is contrasted with its qualities, or Sifat, which are described by the divine names (Asma al-Husna). From this absolute unity, the next stage, Wahdat (Divinized Oneness), arises, followed by Wahdaniyat (Manifest Oneness). As Pir Zia notes, "all three of these words are just permutations of one root, which is Wahda, meaning one." In Wahdaniyat, the first creation emerges: the great soul (Ruh al-A'zam), also called the 'cosmic person' (al-Shakhs al-Kabir). The descent continues through Arwah (the world of souls), Ajsam (the world of mental bodies or minds), and finally culminates in Insan, the plane of physical embodiment. This entire sequence is not a historical event but a perpetual, ongoing process of manifestation from the source.

snippets.title: The Unfolding of the Self and the Universe
snippets.body: The Sufi teaching on the emergence of the seven leading divine names provides a map that describes two parallel processes: the creation of the universe and the formulation of the individual self. This framework suggests that our personal development mirrors the cosmic unfolding. We begin in a state of pure being, Al-Hayy (The Living), akin to the womb. Then comes awareness, Al-Alim (The Knowing), as we begin to recognize the world around us. This is followed by the discovery of our own power and agency, Al-Qadir (The Powerful), like a child learning they can affect their environment. Finally, this power gives rise to will and desire, Al-Murid (The Desiring). "Each of us has a hand in the making of this world. That's no small thing. It's our participation in the power which is the power of all powers." This perspective reveals that our individual lives are not separate from the grand cosmic drama but are an intimate and active part of it.

snippets.title: The Continuous Unfolding of Creation
snippets.body: In Sufi metaphysics, the planes of being—from Ahadiyat (Essential Oneness) to Insan (Physical Embodiment)—are not merely a historical account of a past creation event. Instead, they describe a dynamic, ongoing process. The universe is in a state of perpetual creation, constantly flowing from its source. This challenges the linear, temporal framework we typically use to understand existence. As Pir Zia cautions, we must not think of these stages as events that occurred at a single point in time. He asks, "How can something suddenly come out of infinity? If it was going to happen, why should it happen at one time rather than another?" The answer lies in seeing creation as a continuous emanation, an eternal present. "We're speaking of the ocean and its waves, perpetual creation." This perspective shifts our understanding from a static universe to a living, breathing reality that is constantly being renewed and manifested from the divine essence in every moment.

snippets.title: Divine Nostalgia: The Creative Impulse
snippets.body: The impulse for creation, according to Sufi thought, stems from a profound longing within the Divine, famously expressed in the hadith, "I was a hidden treasure and I longed to be known." Pir Zia Inayat-Khan introduces a unique term for this creative urge, one his father, Pir Vilayat Inayat Khan, was fond of: 'divine nostalgia.' This is not a longing for a past that was, but a paradoxical yearning of the Absolute for self-manifestation and self-knowledge through creation. 'As my father used to love the word nostalgia... he would say the divine nostalgia began to well up,' Pir Zia explains. This concept reframes the act of creation not as a mechanical process but as an expression of love and a deep, intrinsic desire within the divine nature itself, a continuous welling up that is happening in every moment.

snippets.title: The Subjectivity of Naming the Divine Planes
snippets.body: Sufis throughout history have attempted to map the journey of consciousness as it emerges from the unmanifest. They have given names to these gradations of being, but it's crucial to understand that these terms are somewhat arbitrary. As Pir Zia explains, "the names of the planes are subjective and different thinkers give different terms. We're speaking of states of being beyond description and and beyond denomination." This teaching emphasizes that the labels for these profound states are not rigid doctrines but rather clues or keys. They are pointers toward an experience that transcends language. The goal is not to create a fixed philosophical system but to use these names as meditative tools to access the states they represent, recognizing that the ultimate reality they point to is beyond any single definition.

snippets.title: The Practice of Gazing into Darkness
snippets.body: This practice complements the sun and moon gazing exercises by exploring the light inherent in darkness. Find the darkest possible space in your home, blocking all light sources. Sit in total darkness with your eyes open, trying to avoid blinking as much as possible without causing pain. The key is to simply witness the light that appears to your eyes, not to project or imagine anything. Observe the grains of light, their patterns, colors, waves, and ripples with fine attention. This practice demonstrates that "what we call darkness is just a certain portion of the spectrum of illumination in its various modes. Darkness is also a kind of light." The sense of spatial distance dissolves, and the vision might be happening in your retina, your brain, or a disembodied mind. After about ten minutes, take a moment to sense how this act of attention has affected your state of being.

snippets.title: Knocking on the Door of the Latifas with the Breath
snippets.body: The subtle centers within us, known as latifas, are like sacred portals to deeper states of being, and our approach to them is paramount. To open these gateways, especially within the body, we use the breath as a gentle knock. It's akin to approaching the home of someone you hold in great affection and esteem; "you're not going to bang on the door, you're going to gently, gently knock and let the, you know, the cadence of your knock express something of your greeting to the one living inside." This sensitivity in our breath, attuned to each specific subtle space, is crucial. Whether connecting with the passionate heart (Qalb) or the luminous soul (Ruh), our mindful breathing becomes a profound greeting, allowing these divine qualities to softly awaken and manifest within our experience.

3.title: Fikr & Zikr — Remembrance
3.subtitle: Silent and Spoken Remembrance of the One
3.intro: Fikr is the silent remembrance borne on the breath; Zikr is the spoken remembrance that resounds in the heart. Together they form the continuous thread of presence that contemplation gathers into a single luminous point.

snippets.title: Guided Zikr Practice — Ya Hayyu
snippets.body:
# Guided Zikr Practice — Ya Hayyu

## The Practice

Invocation of the divine name Al-Hayy through its Ya-form: "Ya Hayyu" (O Living One).

Guided group zikr led by Pir Zia. The class invokes the name together in unison, repeated 101 times.

"Shall we invoke this name now? Al-Hayy."

## Closing Words

"The livingness in you is no different than the livingness in the Earth, which is no different than the eternal livingness which is in the divine being."

"When you're ready, if you would like, we can open our eyes again."

## Context

This is the first guided zikr practice of the Contemplation series, working with Al-Hayy — the first of the seven Ummahat (mother names). The practice moves from intellectual understanding of Al-Hayy into direct experiential invocation through the repetition of the sacred name.

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*
snippets.session: Suluk Academy 2026 Contemplation P1 Class 1 / March 27, 2026 / Pir Zia Inayat-Khan

snippets.title: Al-Hayy: Contemplating The Living
snippets.body: The first of the seven mother names is Al-Hayy, The Living. This name exemplifies how sacred words embody the quality they designate; the very sound of reciting "Hayy" creates a surge of livingness in the breath. It is the first name because before anything can be or do, it must first live. We often mistakenly equate life with physical embodiment, but this is only one mode of existence. Life is innate and perpetual, existing on subtle, mental, and angelic planes long before our incarnation and long after our physical body dissolves. The profound truth is that "Life never dies. Death dies, life lives." Contemplating Al-Hayy invites us to sink into the ground of life that is more ancient than our current form and to recognize the felicity in the sheer, inalienable livingness of the soul, which is one with the eternal life of the divine being.

snippets.title: Considering Your Responsibility Sacred
snippets.body: The chivalric rule, "My conscientious self, consider your responsibility sacred," is a powerful contemplation on personal agency. It reminds us that we are free to sculpt our lives rather than merely reacting to circumstances. This requires distinguishing our true responsibility, our dharma, from the expectations others may project onto us. The real question is about haqq, the right that is due to each being and, crucially, to our own self. To be true to oneself is to be true to one's purpose. "At the end of the day, we're not going to be asked about something that someone else was responsible for. We'll be asked about what we were responsible for." This seriousness is not about solemnity but about sincerity, care, and devoted attention to our sacred duty, which is the holy of holies contained within our heart, the energy given for our life's work.

snippets.title: The Test of Dharma: The Story of Yudhisthira and the Dog
snippets.body: The story of Yudhisthira from the Mahabharata offers a profound lesson on the nature of dharma, or sacred responsibility. After a long and arduous pilgrimage where he loses his entire family, Yudhisthira is left with only a dog as his companion. When a celestial chariot arrives to take him to his final destination, the angel forbids the dog from entering. Despite his exhaustion and the promise of release, Yudhisthira refuses to abandon his friend. At that moment, the dog transforms, revealing its true identity. "And the dog revealed, 'I am Dharma. I am your Dharma. And you were true to me.'" This moving story illustrates that our true responsibility is often tested in our relationships and our commitment to those who depend on us. It is not an abstract concept but a lived reality of faithfulness, loyalty, and integrity, even at great personal cost.

snippets.title: Entering Malakut: Guided Contemplation as Invitation
snippets.body: A specific method of Muraqaba, or contemplation, involves being led into a landscape within *Malakut*, the imaginal realm. This is done through a narrative and music, which serve as an invitation into a particular kind of space. Unlike concentration where the experience is prescribed, here the entry is an invitation, and what unfolds is self-arising. The teacher explains, "we usher ourselves into a certain space within Malakut, but what unfolds there is, is self-arising in as much as Malakut is a real place." The intention guides us to this realm, but once there, we are free to make our way through the space and are shown what we are shown. We become open to the thoughts, ideas, and presences that are disclosed to us. This practice acknowledges that the imaginal realm is a real place, not just a projection of our own minds, allowing for genuine discovery and insight.

snippets.title: Winter's Renewal: Touching the Root of Being
snippets.body: The guided contemplation uses the metaphor of a winter landscape to explore inner renewal. The narrative evokes the musty scent of the earth, the hibernation of creatures, and seeds germinating underground. This imagery encourages us to step back, lie low, and touch the root of our being before stepping forward again. This is not an escape from life's challenges but a way of fostering renewal by anticipating action. The practice suggests that to be renewed, one sometimes has to "skim rock bottom." The narrative guides, "You are touching upon the root of your being. You will notice that, likened to the earth, it is profuse in its generosity, unrelenting in its benevolence in resorbing our pollution and recycling it into something beautiful." By plunging deep into the springheads of our being, we can haul new ideas, fresh resolve, and a new zest for life to the surface, emerging restored, recharged, and re-enchanted.

snippets.title: The Closing Prayer (Salat): Recognizing the One in All Holy Names
snippets.body: The closing prayer, or Salat, articulates a core universalist teaching by invoking the One Being through the names and forms of diverse religious traditions. It begins by addressing the 'Most gracious sovereign, messenger, messiah, and savior of humanity,' acknowledging the divine light and spirit of guidance as the Alpha and Omega. The prayer asks for the ability to see this one presence in all its manifestations, explicitly naming figures from multiple faiths. "Allow us to recognize thee in all thy holy names and forms, as Sita and Rama, as Radha and Krishna... as Sarah, Hagar, and Abraham... as Mary and Jesus, as Khadija and Muhammad, and many other names and forms known and unknown to the world." This powerful invocation affirms that the same divine reality is perceived through different cultural and religious lenses, with the ultimate hope that the message of God will reach far and wide, 'illuminating and making the whole of humanity one single family in the parenthood of God.'

snippets.title: Untangling Our Desires to Find the Divine Impulse
snippets.body: The spiritual work of Suluk is not about rejecting our human nature but about understanding its sacred origins and refining its expression. The divine qualities we inherit have a tendency to become tangled up within us. As "Murshid said that every desire, every human desire, has at its origin a divine impulse... But that ray, that expression of a divine quality has become confused in us, narrowed down, contorted, tangled up." This does not mean all our urges are enlightened, but that behind even the most confused desire is a providential ray of the divine. The work, then, is one of untangling. It involves taking the whole of our experience, finding the original threads of divine qualities, untying the knots of our conditioning, and weaving them into a beautiful, coherent expression of our true self. It is a process of transformation, not rejection.

snippets.title: Cosmic Recapitulation in the Womb
snippets.body: The grand cosmic process of creation is mirrored and recapitulated in the intimate development of the human embryo. In the womb, an embryo floats in amniotic fluid, its primary sensation being that of life itself, a state of pure being that echoes the divine quality of Al-Hayy, the Living. The Arabic word for womb is 'rahm,' which is the root for the divine names of compassion, Rahman and Rahim. As Pir Zia notes, "Actually, you know the womb is, the word in Arabic is rahm, rahm. Rahman and Rahim come from this. The compassion, the mercy that brought forth this world." As the embryo's brain and nervous system develop, a knowingness emerges, reflecting the quality of Al-'Alim, the Knower. This principle, where individual development mirrors evolutionary and cosmic history, shows how the entire arc of the cosmos is reinscribed within each of us.

snippets.title: Weaving an Exquisite Tapestry of Divine Qualities
snippets.body: The spiritual path is a creative act of weaving the threads of our being into a work of art. The divine qualities we carry within us can often feel like a confused and tangled mess, but the raw material is sacred. The work is not to discard the threads but to untangle them and arrange them beautifully. This metaphor highlights the purpose of our existence. As Pir Zia states, "The same quantity of thread could either be a tangled jumble or an exquisite tapestry. The divine qualities are seeking their expression in this universe. This universe was made for this. We were born for this." This is the true reason for engaging in a path like Suluk. It is a venue for the conscious participation in reality's ultimate project: the articulation and glorification of the divine nature through our unique lives.

snippets.title: Ontogeny Recapitulates Phylogeny: The Cosmos in the Womb
snippets.body: The development of a human being in the womb is not merely a biological process but a profound echo of the entire cosmic story. This mirroring is captured in the scientific and philosophical principle that "Ontogeny recapitulates phylogeny." This idea suggests that the development of an individual organism (ontogeny) replays the evolutionary history of its species (phylogeny). In this teaching, the concept is expanded to a spiritual and cosmic scale. The journey of the embryo, from a state of pure livingness (Al-Hayy) to developing a nervous system and brain that facilitates knowingness (Al-'Alim), mirrors the emergence of the cosmos itself. As Pir Zia explains, "The way we grow in the womb, some would say recapitulates the whole story of human emergence and that process recapitulates the whole arc of the cosmos, which itself echoes the dawning of the divine intelligence." Each individual life, therefore, reinscribes within itself the grand narrative of divine self-revelation.

snippets.title: The Ultimate Gift of Ya Wahhab: Nearness to the Giver
snippets.body: While the grace of Ya Wahhab, the Bestower, can manifest as worldly blessings or new opportunities, its most profound dimension is spiritual. The practice reminds us that the greatest gift is not the gift itself, but the nearness to the Giver that it signifies. The true essence of this divine bestowal is an inner transformation that touches every level of our being. It is a process of becoming a clearer vessel for the divine attributes. "The gift of Wahhab is ultimately the gift of nearness to the Giver. While it may manifest as worldly blessings, its true essence is a spiritual transformation. The greatest gift is the purification of the Nafs, the awakening of the Qalb, and the illumination of the Ruh." This perspective shifts the focus of our longing from external outcomes to internal refinement: the purification of the ego-self (Nafs), the awakening of the spiritual heart (Qalb), and the illumination of the pure spirit (Ruh). This is the unearned grace that truly nourishes the soul on its journey home.

snippets.title: Ya Sabur: The Patient Endurance of Spiritual Struggle
snippets.body: The quality of Sabur, or patience, is the foundation of the entire spiritual journey, known as Mujahada or spiritual struggle. It is far from a passive resignation; rather, it is an active and courageous endurance. It is the capacity to hold firm in our practice day after day, especially when we see no immediate results or when the path feels difficult. This steadfastness is what allows us to polish the mirror of the heart and to face the inner resistance of the ego without being defeated. "Sabur, patience, is the very substance of Mujahada, the spiritual struggle. It is the steadfastness required to remain present with oneself, to witness the agitations of the lower self without being swept away by them." Without this quality, the path is impossible. The story of the saint who meditated for forty years until the wall before him became transparent illustrates the immense power of this unwavering commitment, which prepares the soul to receive the boundless gifts that the Divine has in store.

snippets.title: The Story of the Wali Who Waited Forty Years
snippets.body: Pir Zia shares a classic Sufi parable illustrating the ultimate fruit of Sabur — patience held across decades until the divine opening finally arrives. The tale demonstrates that the vessel of one's being must be prepared through unwavering steadfastness before the immense gift of Wahhab can be received. "There is a story of a great Wali, a friend of God, who sat in Muraqabah for forty years facing a wall, waiting for an opening. Day after day, he practiced with unwavering Sabur. One day, the opening came, and the wall before him became transparent, and he could see the mysteries of the Malakut, the angelic world." This story brings into focus how the sequence of Sabur → Fattah → Wahhab plays out not just across a single meditation session but across a lifetime of devoted practice. The wall itself becomes a teacher: the very obstacle the Wali faced was, through the grace of Fattah, transformed into a window onto the Malakut. His patience, the story teaches, was not passive endurance but active preparation — the forging of a receptacle worthy of what Wahhab had in store.

snippets.title: Nizam un Nisa — The Saint of Sabur and Ishq
snippets.body: Pir Zia introduces Nizam un Nisa, a disciple of Hazrat Nizamuddin Auliya, whose life embodies the teaching of this class: patient, loving service as the ground in which divine love ripens. She is also the namesake of Nizam un Nisa (Husain), the instructor for Part II of Contemplation, giving this story a special significance in the arc of the book. "The life of the great woman saint Nizam un Nisa, a disciple of Hazrat Nizamuddin Auliya, is a beautiful example of Sabur and Ishq. She served her Murshid with selfless devotion for many years, performing the most menial tasks in the Khankah. Her patient, loving service purified her heart to such a degree that she became one of the great spiritual lights of her time, a channel for the divine gifts of Wahhab to all who came to her." Her story offers a complete picture of how the Wazifa of Ya Fattah, Ya Wahhab, Ya Sabur unfolds through a whole human life: Sabur as the patient service in the Khankah, Fattah as the gradual opening of the heart through that service, and Wahhab as the stream of divine gifts that eventually flowed through her to others.

snippets.title: The Marriage of Zikr (Remembrance) and Fikr (Contemplation)
snippets.body: To fully engage with the divine names and allow their spiritual grace to penetrate deeply within us, we are called to bring both our hearts and minds into the practice. It is not enough merely to repeat the sacred sounds; we must also contemplate their profound implications for our lives and the greater cosmos. "It is beneficial to combine the practice of Zikr, the remembrance of the divine names, with Fikr, contemplation on their meanings. As we repeat 'Ya Fattah,' we can contemplate the nature of opening in our lives and in the cosmos... This marriage of sound and meaning deepens the practice..." This conscious fusion ensures the wisdom of the names permeates fully into our hearts and minds, allowing us to move beyond mere recitation. By uniting sound and meaning, we cultivate a profound, experiential connection that transforms our entire being and illuminates our path.

snippets.title: The Murshid's Lesson on Experiential Knowledge
snippets.body: Sufi cosmology uses specific names for the planes of being, not as rigid philosophical concepts, but as meditative keys for direct experience. Pir Zia shares a story about his grandfather, Hazrat Inayat Khan, whose teacher specialized in the philosophy of Ibn al-Arabi. Initially, the teacher spoke only of ordinary things. When he finally began to speak of metaphysics, Hazrat Inayat Khan prepared to take notes. Seeing this, the teacher immediately changed the subject. As Pir Zia recounts, "Hazrat took out his notebook to take notes and the Murshid went back to speaking about the trees and milk and things like that." This powerful lesson underscored that true understanding comes not from intellectual accumulation but from direct, inner realization. The names of the planes were meant to be 'entered' through meditation, not just cataloged.

snippets.title: The Fiery Heart: Born of Sympathy and a Life Deeply Lived
snippets.body: The latifa of the Heart, or Qalb, is a subtle center approached through the space of the physical heart on the left side of the chest. Its element is fire. The heart's flowering is a consequence of the maturation of the Nafs (the personal self). It is born specifically of sympathy, when the Nafs moves beyond its competitive and oppositional nature to feel for others. "When feeling bursts the bounds of the ego and becomes cosmic, becomes sympathetic, becomes open to the inspiration that fuels poetry and music, then the heart is at last blossoming." The Qalb is characterized by a rawness that comes from a life deeply lived through both pain and grace. It holds passion, forgiveness, and longing. This fiery center is distinct from the earthy Nafs, the airy Khafi, and the watery Ruh, representing a passionate engagement with the cosmos.

snippets.title: The Luminous Soul: The Flowing Freshness of Ruh
snippets.body: Complementing the fiery heart (Qalb) is the watery soul, or Ruh. In this teaching, soul refers to Ruh, not Nafs. The Ruh is associated with the right side of the chest, and its element is water. Its Persian synonym, Ravan, means "flowing." While the heart is raw and passionate, the soul is luminous, gentle, and fresh. "The soul is fresh-sprung, you might say, from the source. There's a nascency in it, a fresh, sheer, simple immediacy, whereas the Khafi represents the sobriety that comes after intoxication." This freshness of the Ruh is what strengthens the heart and can stir it from anesthesia. It offers a counterpoint to the heart's intensity, bringing a quality of pure breath and dawn light. Together, the fiery Qalb and the watery Ruh form a complementary pair, essential for a balanced inner life.

snippets.title: The Subtle Centers as Portals to States of Being
snippets.body: The subtle centers, or latifas, form a quaternity of being: the earthy Nafs (personal self), the airy Khafi (higher vision), the fiery Qalb (passionate heart), and the watery Ruh (luminous soul). It is important to understand that "these modes of being are not restricted to one location, but these organs that we describe, these Latif are entry points into a certain, um, state of being." While we associate them with specific places in the body—the belly, forehead, left chest, and right chest—these are best understood as portals. Different Sufi systems may even identify them in different places. The key is that by focusing our attention and breath on these locations, we can access the corresponding states of consciousness. We open these portals by knocking gently with the breath, approaching with affection and esteem, allowing the cadence of our breath to express a greeting to the quality of being that resides within.

snippets.title: A Contemplative Journey into a Fiery Landscape
snippets.body: This contemplation invites a journey into a fiery landscape, a virtual world of pure, exhilarating phantasm. A caution is advised for anyone with trauma related to fire. The journey begins by peering through a kaleidoscope into worlds of light, imagining spirals of light, beings of light, and fireworks. You then imagine becoming a firecracker, exploding and diffracting into this landscape. The perspective shifts to soaring above clouds at sunrise, experiencing the ecstasy of buoyancy. A realization dawns that these are the "blueprints of the forms of the world," like temples of light inhabited by beings of light. The contemplation culminates in observing its effect upon you. "As you gaze in the mirror, you cannot believe what you see. You are transformed beyond recognition, radiant, glowing." You might even discover an angelic countenance behind your own features, a revelation of your transformed self.

snippets.title: The Watery Soul (Ruh) and its Cognates
snippets.body: The soul, or Ruh, is described as one of a complementary pair of subtle centers, standing opposite the fiery heart (Qalb). While the Nafs is earthy and the Khafi is airy, the Ruh is watery. Its nature is to flow, a quality reflected in its Persian synonym, Ravan, which means 'flowing.' The etymological roots of the word connect it to breath across languages, such as the Sanskrit 'Atman' and its German cognate 'atem,' meaning 'the breath, pure breath.' In contrast to the raw, passionate heart, the soul is luminous, gentle, and watery. It is characterized by a unique freshness and immediacy. "The soul is fresh-sprung, you might say, from the source. There's a nascency in it, a fresh, sheer, simple immediacy." This quality is distinct from the sobriety of the Khafi, representing a pure, nascent state of being.

snippets.title: Embracing Pain to Open the Heart
snippets.body: The heart, or Qalb, carries our store of emotions, which can lead to it being closed down as a protective strategy. However, this avoidance leads to greater problems. The teaching emphasizes that true opening requires facing what is stored within. "By refusing, um, pain, one dooms oneself to suffering." Sooner or later, one must accept the pain in the heart, allow it, and feel it fully. This personal pain becomes a gateway to a universal experience, allowing one to sense the pain present in all of creation. In the same moment of deep connection to universal suffering, one can also "encounter the joy that is equally omnipresent through a mood of deep solidarity with all of the beings." It is the fresh, luminous quality of the Ruh, the soul, that ultimately strengthens the heart and can awaken it from this anesthesia.

snippets.title: Guided Zikr of the Syllables Ha and Hu
snippets.body: Friends, the time has come to move to the next stage of *ittisal* in our practice. We have been working with the syllable "Ha," which you feel right here in the chest, connecting to your physical and etheric body. The next stage introduces the syllable "U" (or "Hu"), which vibrates higher up, lifting energy through the throat into the skull, and aligning with the mental or astral being. This shift in vibration allows a profound identification with the universal psyche. So, let’s do a few times: "Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. Ha. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U. U." This guided recitation deepens our awareness of interconnectedness, animating and sustaining our body and mind, as we come to understand that our individual mind is a part of the vast, intergalactic universal mind.

4.title: Wazifa — Working with the Names
4.subtitle: The Ninety-Nine Names as Instruments of Transformation
4.intro: The Names of God are not mere epithets but living attributes of the Divine. To repeat a Name with attention is to invite that quality to take root in the soul. This chapter gathers the wazifas given during the retreat — each a key to a particular door.

snippets.title: Wazifa — Murshid's Complete Teaching on the Practice
snippets.body:
# Wazifa — Murshid's Complete Teaching on the Practice

## What Wazifa Is

"One of the practices that is central to the inner school is the practice of Wazifa. And Wazifa, as you may know, means an assignment, an assignment that is given to you and that you keep up with dedication, a daily practice. A name that is entrusted to you, a divine name given into your care."

"That this name has come down through revelation, has been passed down through the silsila, and now is given to you as a trust to, yes, to say, say it with your tongue, certainly, but more than that, to enter its field. When you have a Wazifa for that time, you enter the field of that name. You dedicate your life to beholding God through the portal of this particular name."

"A door is open to you. And as soon as that Wazifa is given and accepted, yes, you say the name diligently each day in the morning, or with some Wazifas it will be in the evening. But more than that, you live your life in the continuous presence of that divine attribute. You become the 'abd, you become the servant of that name."

## Murshid's Teaching — Read in Full by Pir Zia

"Every spoken word is a re-echo of thought and every thought is the re-echo of an emotion. And as the activity from within manifests on the surface, so the word spoken takes its reverse process. It is reproduced on the mind, and from the mind, it is reproduced on the heart, the factor of feeling. That which comes from within comes involuntarily, and what enters within from without enters voluntarily."

"Therefore, Wazifa is taught by Sufis as a voluntary production of a certain thing on the mind and in the heart. Mind being powerful to produce that which is impressed upon it from within and without. As many times as a word is repeated, even in a whisper, so many records it has produced in one's mind and in the heart."

### Principles of Practice (from Murshid)

| Principle | Guidance |
|-----------|----------|
| **Numbers** | Must be in accordance with the law of numbers — set count, regular rhythm; without consideration of numbers, rhythm and balance are lost |
| **Moon phase** | Commence during the rise of the moon (progressive trend); waning moon brings upset and confusion |
| **Place** | Same place every day creates a growing atmosphere |
| **Time** | Same time every day — invisible forces get accustomed to meet and help; without a set time, it becomes difficult to gather them |
| **Purity** | Cleanliness of body, purity of mind, fragrance, beautiful thoughts give quicker result |
| **Diet** | Flesh food is sometimes undesirable for Wazifa; disturbances of mind make it fruitless |
| **Character** | Weakness of character and an impious life ruins the life of the one who repeats Wazifa |
| **Secrecy** | Keep the Wazifa secret and its effect secret; pride of repeating should be avoided |
| **Authorization** | Must be taught by a blessed soul or permission taken from a holy person — produces a thousandfold effect |

## The Living Experience — Pir Zia's Commentary

"Thoughts become words, and very often this happens without intentionality. But if we have a clear intention, we may choose a word, and not only a word, a name, not only a name, a divine name. Let it be given or let it be blessed, and then repeat it at the same time every day, as much as possible in the same place every day."

"And then the word becomes a thought, thought becomes a feeling, body, mind, heart, soul, all become aligned in the field of the quality which is an emanation of the divine nature. We do this without fanfare, it's done quietly, no one else knows."

"Beginning with the waxing moon, and when there is consistency, then the magnetism of the practice builds up. You're joined by angels and benevolent jinn. You're surrounded by whirling thought beings that are drawn to the name because they too are entrusted with it."

"And when you recite the name, you'll also recognize that name in others who are entrusted with it and those who simply embody it in one fashion or another. That name will show itself on all sides. The name will become a world."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*
snippets.session: Suluk Academy 2026 Contemplation P1 Class 1 / March 27, 2026 / Pir Zia Inayat-Khan

snippets.title: Meditating on a Stone: The Example of Pir Dahan
snippets.body: The first stage of our spiritual journey is the Earth element, representing deep concentration and profound stillness. To master concentration is to become entirely unperturbed by the moving world around us. "Sometimes the mystics meditate on stones," Pir Zia notes, emphasizing the power of such groundedness. "There's a very special place in California where Hazrat Inayat Khan sat on a stone. It's called Pir Dahan, the mouth of the Pir. You can still feel his vibrations there." This location serves as a powerful reminder of what true concentration entails. "A stone carries an abiding vibration down through the years because its consciousness isn't scattered. It's very rooted, very stable." By emulating the stone at Pir Dahan, we develop the capacity for unperturbed attention, laying a stable foundation before we allow the mind to move in contemplation.

snippets.title: Becoming the Servant (Abd) of the Name
snippets.body: When a divine name is given as a Wazifa, it is not simply a word to be recited; it is an assignment to enter its sacred field. It is a trust, a divine name given into your care. As soon as a Wazifa is given and accepted, you dedicate your life to beholding God through the portal of this particular name. Yes, you say the name diligently each day, but "But more than that, you live your life in the continuous presence of that divine attribute. You become the Abd, you become the servant of that name." This profound practice means allowing the divine quality of the name to permeate your entire existence, transforming your inner and outer life until the name becomes your world, and its essence is embodied in your every thought, word, and action.

snippets.title: The Primal Intelligence Before Consciousness
snippets.body: A distinction can be made between intelligence and consciousness. Ibn Sina's 'floating man' thought experiment illustrates this: imagine a person suspended without any sensory input. Gradually, mental activity would abate, and consciousness would return to its ground state of pure intelligence. This is a primal, innate self-awareness that exists without a subject-object distinction. Consciousness, as we typically experience it, arises from this ground. As Murshid Inayat Khan taught, "Intelligence is consciousness in its innate condition. When intelligence is presented with an object, then there is a subject and an object and a knowledge intervening between them. And this is what we would call consciousness." A child's development reflects this journey, moving from the ensconced, innate intelligence of infancy to a differentiated consciousness of self in relation to the world, a process that recapitulates the cosmic journey of becoming.

snippets.title: Zikr Practice: Invoking Ya 'Alimu, The All-Knowing
snippets.body: The session concludes with a prolonged and immersive Zikr practice, focusing on the divine name Al-'Alim, the All-Knowing. This contemplative repetition, or remembrance, serves to internalize the quality of divine intelligence that was the subject of the preceding teaching. By chanting the name, participants are invited to move beyond intellectual understanding and into a direct, heart-centered experience of this divine attribute. The rhythmic invocation of 'Ya 'Alimu' creates a meditative state, allowing the consciousness to be saturated with the reality of the Knower. The practice is a deep dive into the ocean of divine awareness, a way of polishing the mirror of the heart so that it may reflect the boundless knowledge of the One. The simple yet profound phrase, "Ya 'Alimu, ya 'Alimu, ya 'Alimu..." becomes a vessel carrying the practitioner from the shore of the finite self to the infinite presence of the All-Knowing reality.

snippets.title: The Power of Consistent Practice and Community
snippets.body: Effective spiritual practice relies on both personal discipline and communal support. The quality of Sabur is not only about waiting for a grand opening but also about the daily commitment to show up for the practice, regardless of mood or circumstance. This steady rhythm builds spiritual momentum and is a prayer in itself. "Consistency is key in this practice. It is better to practice for a short time every day than for a long time once a week. This daily return to the inner sanctuary builds a momentum." Furthermore, the effect of this practice is greatly amplified when undertaken within a spiritual community, or Khankah. When a group of hearts focus their attention (Tawajjuh) on the same divine qualities, a powerful energetic field is created. This collective intention and shared spiritual grace (Baraka) can help facilitate openings for each individual, providing profound support and encouragement for everyone on the path.

snippets.title: The Silsila: A Living Practice Imbued with Baraka
snippets.body: On the path of Suluk, a Wazifa given by a Murshid (spiritual guide) is more than just a personal technique or formula. It is a key that connects the student to a vast spiritual inheritance. The practice is imbued with the living presence and grace of the entire lineage of teachers who have transmitted it. This chain of transmission is known as the Silsila. When we engage with such a practice, we are not working in isolation; we are supported by the collective spiritual energy of the saints and masters who walked this path before us. "It is a key, a tool for transformation, but it is not just a formula. It is a living practice, imbued with the Baraka, the spiritual grace, of the entire Silsila, the chain of transmission." This connection provides immense support, infusing our efforts with a Baraka, or spiritual grace, that flows through the lineage, helping to open what is closed and sustain us through trials.

snippets.title: The Closing Prayers: Salat and Khatum
snippets.body: The session concludes with the rising for prayer, a core element of the shared practice. The prayers begin with the Salat, addressing the 'Most gracious sovereign, messenger, Messiah, savior of humanity,' and recognizing the divine presence in all holy names and forms, from Sita and Rama to Mary and Jesus, and Khadija and Muhammad. This is followed by the Khatum prayer, an invocation to the 'perfection of love, harmony, and beauty' to open our hearts and disclose the divine light within our souls. The practice culminates in a final blessing for all participants. "May your heart be filled with heavenly joy. May your soul be illumined with divine light. May your spirit unfold the divine message." These prayers serve to seal the contemplative work and extend its blessings outwards.

5.title: Contemplation Practices
5.subtitle: Holding the One Reality in the Heart
5.intro: At the center of the Suluk stands contemplation itself — mushahada, muraqaba, the gaze of the witness, the abiding in presence. These practices train the heart to hold a single reality until the veil between seeker and sought dissolves.

snippets.title: Aman and Dar al-Aman — The Realm of Sanctuary
snippets.body:
# Aman and Dar al-Aman — The Realm of Sanctuary

Pir Zia opened the Contemplation series with an extended reflection on the state of the world and the community's response — holding prayer for Suluk members in conflict zones.

## The Interior Source of Sanctuary

"Aman isn't just something that arises outside, it arises outside because it begins in the interior life. And our intention is to create the Dar al-Aman, the realm of sanctuary beginning in our own hearts. We do this through the consideration that we have for all of God's creatures. The consideration that we show to each other in Suluk, and the intention to make every thought, every word, and every action a stone in that temple of sanctuary that we're building."

## Humanity as One Family

"I know that the members of this class who are very near to falling bombs, flying drones, you've been in my prayers, and I know we've all been holding this prayer. And others have family who are in dangerous places, endangered places. And in fact, all of us have family living in danger because humanity is one family. We're all interrelated."

"And what happens to the least protected, the most aggressed against, happens really to all of us. So we keep lifting up this prayer and let's care for each other. Let's be a community that demonstrates, that embodies kindredness. We can't control what happens throughout the world, but we can create sanctuary amongst those we know, those we meet through the attitude of Aman."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*
snippets.session: Suluk Academy 2026 Contemplation P1 Class 1 / March 27, 2026 / Pir Zia Inayat-Khan

snippets.title: From Concentration to Contemplation — Mujahada to Muraqabah
snippets.body:
# From Concentration to Contemplation — Mujahada to Muraqabah

Pir Zia formally named the transition using the Arabic terms and mapped the four Suluk stages to the four elements.

## The Transition

"Now, we are taking a turn. We're moving from concentration to contemplation. Some of you speak Arabic or languages that use Arabic vocabulary. Concentration is sometimes called mujahada. So we've been practicing mujahada. And what comes next is Muraqabah, contemplation."

"From concentration to contemplation. So over these next few days, we'll begin to develop a sense of what contemplation is, how it differs from concentration. We have to begin with concentration, but the story doesn't end there."

## The Four Stages as Four Elements

| Stage | Arabic | Element | Quality |
|-------|--------|---------|--------|
| **Concentration** | Mujahada | Earth | Heavy, dense, slow — learning to be still like a rock |
| **Contemplation** | Muraqabah | Water | The mind begins to move gracefully, artfully, purposefully |
| **Meditation** | — | Air | (To come) |
| **Realization** | — | Fire | (To come) |

"Earth, as you know, is very heavy, very dense, very slow. It corresponds to concentration. And in our fast moving world where attention is so rare, it's the first medicine. The very first thing to learn is to slow down and be like a rock. Just learn to be fully still, fully silent, fully present."

"And I hope, and I believe, that over the last several months you've had the chance to deepen that capacity for stillness, focus, unperturbed attention. So we begin with concentration, but we don't end there. And after the earth element comes the element of water."

## The Teaching of the Stone — Pir Dahan

"Rocks aren't usually given much consideration, but a rock can teach us a lot. And even spending time with a stone becomes an opportunity to emulate the stillness of the stone. The way it yields to gravity and occupies a single place. Sometimes the mystics meditate on stones."

"There's a very special place in California where Hazrat Inayat Khan sat and meditated on a stone. It's called Pir Dahan, the mouth of the Pir. You can still feel his vibrations there. A stone carries an abiding vibration down through the years because its consciousness isn't scattered. It's very rooted, very stable. And so this is what we've been developing in concentration."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*
snippets.session: Suluk Academy 2026 Contemplation P1 Class 1 / March 27, 2026 / Pir Zia Inayat-Khan

snippets.title: What Contemplation Is — The Mind Moves Gracefully
snippets.body:
# What Contemplation Is — The Mind Moves Gracefully

## From Stillness to Purposeful Movement

"Having been still, having been focused, focused on one object, now we're going to begin to move. The mind is going to begin to move. We didn't want the mind to move until we were present and focused because it would just move randomly, it would just react to stimuli."

"But now that we've drawn our attention in and stabilized it and deepened it, now we can let it unfold, let it find its way. And this is what contemplation does."

"So there will be more to say in the coming days and more to practice in the way of exploring what it means for the mind to move, but not erratically, not randomly, but gracefully, artfully, purposefully. This is Muraqabah."

## From Objects to Qualities — The Architecture of Meaning

"And it means that we're not only going to be focusing on objects, or even the forms of objects, but something subtler still. The quality of something, the character of something, the nature of it. And this is leading us into the architecture of meaning that underlies the visible universe."

"What are the qualities of being that all things manifest in various combinations? These qualities are in fact, in their essence, the attributes of God. And the names of God invoke them."

---
*Kabir's Sufi Knowledge Base · Inayatiyya*
snippets.session: Suluk Academy 2026 Contemplation P1 Class 1 / March 27, 2026 / Pir Zia Inayat-Khan

snippets.title: Closing Contemplation — Scattered Attention and the Holy of Holies
snippets.body:
# Closing Contemplation — Scattered Attention and the Holy of Holies

Pir Zia guided a closing contemplation on acknowledging scattered attention and reconnecting with sacred purpose.

## The Contemplation

"So you could just take a moment and acknowledge the ways in which you've been scattered, caught up in things that aren't really yours to attend to."

"At the time it just seems like a little thing, it was just a little extravagance, a little deviation. But, you know, pitter-patter, splish splash, the drops add up. Each little drop of kind of negligence and distraction accumulates and soon we're drowning in forgetfulness."

"So after noticing all of the distractedness, we could just take a moment and sense, you know, your real work, what you're responsible for, even if it's hard. Just the sacredness of your sacred duty. How it's a holy thing, actually. Maybe more holy than, you know, artifacts in temples or synagogues, mosques, churches. It's the holy of holies contained in your heart, the sacred energy given to you for your opus, your work."

## Closing Recitation

Spoken twice — Pir Zia first, then the class joins:

"My conscientious self, consider your responsibility sacred."

"My conscientious self, consider your responsibility sacred."
